coredata.dc:title: Biomass-derived potential nano-biomaterials: Protein binding, anti-biofilm activity and bio-imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Kosuru, Rekha Yamini
Srinivasan, Venkatesan
Jhonsi, Mariadoss Asha
coredata.dc:description: Deriving carbon dots (CDs) from renewable sources is gaining popularity due to their prevalence, cost effectiveness, improved aqueous solubility, and increased biocompatibility. CDs, which are made up of crystalline graphitized carbon cores and multiple surface groups, are now spawning a lot of attention in the biological domains. Herein, the CDs were derived from dried rose petals using hydrothermal route and were completely characterized utilizing pivotal techniques. The prepared CDs are spherical particles that are approximately 5–6nm in size and have exceptional stability when exposed to pH and light irradiation. The derived CDs are excitation dependent fluorescent in nature with the quantum yield of 6%. Bovine serum albumin (BSA) binding interaction has been studied with CDs and evaluated through Stern−Volmer equation. The fluorescence quenching mechanism of BSA by CDs followed static type, is further confirmed by the lifetime studies. The binding mode of the CDs with BSA has been studied by molecular docking and the computed binding free energy for CDs is calculated to be −9.62 kcal/mol. Antibiofilm activity revealed that CDs were efficient in reducing the biofilm formations in both gram-positive and gram-negative bacterium. Predominantly, CDs used in this study were significant in reducing the biofilms of gram-negative bacterium. Furthermore, the CDs are effectively used as an efficient biological probe for bioimaging studies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass
Carbon nanomaterial
Protein binding interaction
Docking study
Anti-biofilm activity
Bio-imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the CDs
Binding interaction study of CDs with BSA

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: M.A.J. acknowledge BSACIST for the seed grant (Reference No: CSD/CSM/2022/08, Dt: 14–07–2022). K.R.Y. acknowledges Mr. K. Sakthivel, PG student, for his assistance in benchmark experiments. V. S greatly acknowledges Vel Tech Rangarajan Dr. Sagunthala R&D Institute of Science and Technology, Chennai-600 062, India, for providing the SEED fund (File No: VTU SEED (FY-22–23)−07). V.S. also acknowledges Dr. A. Kathiravan, Professor, Department of Chemistry, Centre for Molecular Photoscience, Vel Tech Research Park, for the spectral measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of RCDs (a) HR-SEM, (b) HR-TEM, (c) Histogram plot and (d) ATR-FT-IR spectrum.(The data has already been published [58])
(a) Absorption and emission spectra, (b) Excitation dependent emission spectra and (c) fluorescence lifetime decay of CDs. (The data has already been published [58])
(a) Emission spectra of BSA with CDs and (b) Fluorescence lifetime decay of BSA and BSA with CDs.
(a) Stern-Volmer plot and (b) double-logarithmic plot of the fluorescence quenching of BSA by CDs.

(a) Amino acid residues of BSA binds with CDs with in the 4Å region (Stick view) (b) 2D schematic representation of interactions between CDs and BSA, (c) Surface view representation of BSA showing the cavity where CDs goes and interact with amino acids and (d) Docking conformation of CDs-BSA (ribbon view).
Effect of RCDs on Biofilm cells of (a) A.caviae (well plate) and (b) S.mutans (well plate).
Effect of RCDs on biofilm forms of A.caviae and S.mutans.
Bright field and fluorescence images of A. caviae and S.mutans incubated with RCDs under different excitation wavelengths.

head.ce:title: Biomass-derived potential nano-biomaterials: Protein binding, anti-biofilm activity and bio-imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biotechnology, SRM Arts and Science College, Kattankulathur 603 203, Chengalpattu, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Biotechnology, SRM Arts and Science College, Kattankulathur, Chengalpattu, Tamil Nadu 603 203, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Vel Tech Rangarajan Dr.Sagunthala R&D Institute of Science and Technology, Avadi, Chennai 600 062, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Vel Tech Rangarajan Dr.Sagunthala R&D Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, B. S. Abdur Rahman Crescent Institute of Science and Technology, Chennai 600 048, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: B. S. Abdur Rahman Crescent Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cDepartment of Chemistry, B. S. Abdur Rahman Crescent Institute of Science and Technology, Chennai, Tamil Nadu 600 048, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Deriving carbon dots (CDs) from renewable sources is gaining popularity due to their prevalence, cost effectiveness, improved aqueous solubility, and increased biocompatibility. CDs, which are made up of crystalline graphitized carbon cores and multiple surface groups, are now spawning a lot of attention in the biological domains. Herein, the CDs were derived from dried rose petals using hydrothermal route and were completely characterized utilizing pivotal techniques. The prepared CDs are spherical particles that are approximately 5–6nm in size and have exceptional stability when exposed to pH and light irradiation. The derived CDs are excitation dependent fluorescent in nature with the quantum yield of 6%. Bovine serum albumin (BSA) binding interaction has been studied with CDs and evaluated through Stern−Volmer equation. The fluorescence quenching mechanism of BSA by CDs followed static type, is further confirmed by the lifetime studies. The binding mode of the CDs with BSA has been studied by molecular docking and the computed binding free energy for CDs is calculated to be −9.62 kcal/mol. Antibiofilm activity revealed that CDs were efficient in reducing the biofilm formations in both gram-positive and gram-negative bacterium. Predominantly, CDs used in this study were significant in reducing the biofilms of gram-negative bacterium. Furthermore, the CDs are effectively used as an efficient biological probe for bioimaging studies.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Novel class of fluorescent nanomaterials such as carbon dots (CDs) has attracted a lot of scientific attention recently. CDs are oxygenous carbon-based nanomaterials with surface functional groups that differ in size from 2 to 10 nm and are composed mostly of carbon, oxygen, and hydrogen. Contrary to polymers, traditional organic dyes, metal nanoparticles, and semiconductor quantum dots, CDs have many superior qualities, including low toxicity, good biocompatibility, environmental friendliness, ease of functionalization, high water dispersibility, simple preparation, and photostability [1]. A wide range of applications such as optoelectronics, biosensing/chemo sensing, bio-imaging, thernostic agents and drug carriers, rely heavily on CDs because of their diverse features [2]. A variety of source materials, including those that are natural, chemical, and biological can be used to prepare CDs [3]. Biomass is a significant source of carbon to produce carbon-based nanomaterials, due to its renewability, sustainability, ease of availability, non-toxic nature, and cheap cost. Biomass-based CDs have previously been shown to have a number of benefits over organic-based equivalents, including excellent biocompatibility, economically viable, photostability as well as applied in different emerging fields [4–7]. Due to the presence of the above-mentioned properties, CDs has been proved to be a prospective material to be utilized in biological field when compared with other inorganic semiconductor quantum dots. The conjugation ability of the nanoparticles make them quite interesting because of their larger surface area which enables them in binding to several chemical and biological compounds [8].

Studies on the functions and interactions of proteins are essential for the advancement of life sciences because of their significance in biological function. Such research is important for the finding and screening of pathogens, diagnosis and therapy of novel drug targets [9–17]. Proteins have a dual role in the body, moving a variety of biological molecules such metal ions, amino/fatty acids, bile pigments, hormones and many therapeutic molecules while also preserving proper blood osmotic pressure [18]. Serum protein, one of the key components of protein transport, is widely used in pharmacological and biological research. Among them, BSA is a crucial protein and its 3D structure has been established [19] well. An unique benefit of albumin-based carriers is their minimal toxicity [20], solubility, biocompatibility and biodegradability [21,22], as well as their availability and cost-effectiveness [23]. Since tumor cells prefer to take them up, they might be used as the best choice for delivering therapeutic ligands into cells [24].

In recent days, biofilm-based infections are becoming a major threat to life. Treatment for bacterial infections along with antibiotics has been an age-old method [25]. Upon consistent use of antibiotics, bacterium starts to get adapted to it and develops resistance against the same. In order to overcome this hurdle, recently a mechanism of developing resistance is the biofilm formation of by the bacterium [26]. Both gram negative and gram-positive bacteria has the ability to form biofilms out of which some of the nosocomial pathogens such as P.aeruginosa, S. aureus, S.mutans, A.baumanii, K.pneumomia, E.coli etc. tend to be strong biofilm formers [27]. Recent research revolves around reducing or treating bacterial infections by targeting the biofilm formations and mechanisms involved in it [28]. As of now, development of novel antibacterial agents is of immediate requirement in the healthcare industry. Scientists aim at developing novel antimicrobial agents by involving the advanced field of nanotechnology [29–31].

Bioimaging can be performed with simple and elegant techniques ranging from X-Ray and B-scan ultrasound to advanced techniques like Positron emission computed topography (PET), Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) etc. [32]. Owing to its advantages of facile instrument handling and easy observation, fluorescence imaging is a widely used technique in clinical imaging [33,34]. United States of Food Drug and Administration (US-FDA) has recognized and approved numerous small molecule dyes (e.g., indocyanine green, fluorescein, methylene blue and so on) for the purpose of imaging as they tend to be safe [35,36]. In spite of these characteristics, the above-mentioned dyes tend to get affected in their half-life and quenching property in a microsecond time (µs) scale [37,38]. To overcome these limitations, numerous fluorescence imaging agents like conjugated polymers [39–44], quantum dots [45–48] and other nanocomposite materials [49–54] have been widely developed in the last decade. Therefore, design and development of innovative and efficient antibacterial and strong fluorescent agents with biocompatibility are mostly demanded. Among them, carbon-based nanomaterials, particularly carbon dots, are emerged as potential candidates for both antibacterial and bio-imaging studies. For instance, Kang et al. reported that, CDs derived from natural biomass-Artemisia argyi leaves proved to be a potent antibacterial agent by targeting Gram negative bacterium [55]. Li et al. synthesized bacteria derived fluorescent carbon dots from Bacillus cereus and further applied for selective detection of p-nitrophenol and bio imaging [56]. Mariappan and co-workers prepared carbon quantum dots from Citrus medica fruit and evaluated the antimicrobial and antibiofilm activity against Pseudomonas aeruginosa [57].

From the above deliberation, it is clearly noticeable that carbon dots may be used as a significant fluorescent probe in biological applications. Recently, we have reported the thymol functionalized carbon dots as nanocarriers for anti-rheumatic effects on Freund's complete adjuvant (FCA) induced arthritic rats [58]. In extension of that reported work, the manuscript presented here explored few of other biological applications of CDs such as protein binding interaction, antibacterial agent against pathogenic gram-negative and gram-positive strains and bio imaging probe.

The waste rose petals were bought from a flower shop in Chennai, India. Analytical grade chemicals and solvents were brought from LOBA chemicals (India). Double distilled water was used for all the sample preparation and spectral analysis. In this study, the bacterial strains Aeromonas caviae (MTCC#7725) and Streptococcus mutans (MTCC#497) were employed. The bacterial cells were regularly grown and maintained at 37 °C in LB broth medium.

The absorption data was recorded using a Hitachi U-3310 diode array spectrophotometer and the fluorescence spectra was observed on a Hitachi F-7000 spectrofluorometer. Quinine sulphate (in 0.1 M H2SO4) was used as a standard for calculating the QY of CDs. The Particle shape and size were measured with the help of high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (JEOL JEM-2100 with accelerating voltage of 120kV). Scanning electron microscope (SEM) measurement was performed using a Nova NanoSEM 600 with EDAX systems from FEI company, Netherlands. The functional groups were identified by JASCO FT-IR ATR 6300 spectrometer in the range of 4000–400 cm−1. The fluorescence lifetime was calculated by time-correlated single-photon counting system using a JOBIN-VYON and data analysis was done by DAS-6 software. NMR analyses were performed by BRUKER spectrometer (400MHz for 1H NMR and 100MHz for 13C NMR).

BSA stock solution (1%) was made using phosphate buffered saline (PBS) solution with pH 7.4, and the concentration of BSA was calculated spectrophotometrically by using ε280=44,300 M−1 cm−1 [59].The stock solution was prepared by dissolving CDs in water (1mg/mL). The protein concentration was kept constant (3 µM) throughout the absorption and fluorescence experiments while the carbon dots concentration varied from 1 to 10 µg/mL. The fluorescence quenching analysis were taken in 280 to 500 nm range, with maximum emission intensity observed at 343 nm (λex = 275 nm) and excitation and emission slit width being 5/5nm.

The 3D structure of BSA has been downloaded from the Protein Data Bank (PDB) database (PDB ID-4F5S). The 3D structures of the CDs were prepared in Chem Axon online software. The molecular docking was carried out using CB-Dock software and the obtained docking results were evaluated in Molegro molecular viewer.

Aeromonas cavieae (MTCC # 7725) and Streptococcus mutans (MTCC # 497) biofilms were formed on pre sterilized 96 well plates in triplicates as mentioned by Merritt et al. with minor modifications [60]. Briefly, 10 µL of overnight cell suspensions having 0.1 OD600nm were inoculated in 190 µL of freshly prepared LB broth. RCDs was added according to the indicated concentrations and the plates were incubated for 24h with shaking at 37 °C to obtain the biofilm layer. At the end of 24h incubation the planktonic cells were aspirated and wells along with biofilms were washed twice with 1X PBS and plates were air dried. Then 200 µL of 0.1 % of crystal violet solution was added to all wells and it was incubated for 30 min. At the end of incubation, excess stain was removed by washing the plate with distilled water. The stained cells were then solubilized by adding 200 µL of 30% acetic acid. Finally, the biofilm growth was measured at OD490nm on a Readwell Touch ELISA microplate reader.

A.caviae and S.mutans biofilm cells were incubated at 37 °C for 24h in Luria Bertani broth medium. Both biofilm cells were incubated with 500 μg/mL of buffered RCDs solution for 15min. After incubation, the cells were washed with PBS solution to discard extracellular RCDs probe and kept in PBS to record the fluorescence imaging. Fluorescence images of live cells were attained by confocal microscopy with an excitation wavelength of 488 and 552 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Carbonaceous nonmaterial is derived from the dried waste of rose petals followed by the previous reported work [58]. A straightforward one-pot hydrothermal process is adopted to create CDs from dried biomass (Scheme S1). Typically, 10 g of the waste rose petals were thoroughly cleaned and dried in an oven at 60 °C and crushed into powder. The resulting powder is stirred with 100 mL of the distilled water, then added to a 250 mL Teflon-coated stainless-steel autoclave and heated to 180 °C for 6h in an electrical furnace. The solution was then filtered, centrifuged, and passed through a 0.2μm cellulose syringe filter. Before that unreacted organic molecules were eventually eliminated with the use of dichloromethane washing. The resultant red brown and clear solution is kept for dialysis followed by volume reduction. Final dialyzed product of CDs is dried in a vacuum oven, resulting red crystalline flakes. Biomass-recycled CDs can be seen as the red crystal product. The proposed mechanism of CDs formation is shown in Scheme S2. Fig. 1 a–c shows the characterization data of HR-SEM and HR-TEM reveals that the synthesized CDs show spherical shape of the particles and an average particle size of 5–6 nm. Presence of various functional groups on the surface of CDs is confirmed by FT-IR and NMR data (Fig. 1d, S1&S2). This indicates the typical properties of CDs like water solubility and ability to binding with biomolecules which enabled us to carry out biological related applications. Fig. 2 displays the photophysical properties of CDs and the absorption spectrum of CDs manifested a broad shoulder peak in the range of 300–500nm is due to the π-π* and n–π* transitions (aromatic sp2 graphitic carbon and carbonyl groups). Further, the emission spectrum of CDs depicts the excitation-dependent emission and the calculated average lifetime is 3.8ns from the lifetime analysis is observed.

The stability of CDs is of great importance for the practical applications. Moreover, it is necessary to check the stability by various parameters such as effect of pH and light irradiation time on the fluorescence intensity of CDs. Fig. S3a shows the effect of pH ranging from 1 to 14 on the fluorescence intensity of CDs. The maximum emission intensity is observed at pH 7. Concurrently, the fluorescence intensity of CDs is varied significantly when the pH of the solution is either more acidic (pH below 3) or more alkaline (pH above 8), whereas the fluorescence intensity did not significantly changed when exposed to pH of 3, 5, and 7. This result clearly reveals that the prepared CDs is sensitive to pH of the medium. At the same time the emission intensity of CDs is highly stable at the pH range of 3–9 which is a good sign to employ the CDs for sensing as well as biological applications. Further, the effect of light irradiation on the fluorescence intensity of CDs has also been investigated (Fig. S3b). The photostability of CDs was demonstrated through continuous UV light (365nm) irradiation. The fluorescence intensity of CDs did not significantly vary by light irradiation time from 0 to 60 min. To check the stability of the solution, it has been kept for more than three months and it was observed that it was not contaminated and appeared to be a clean and clear solution. The above-mentioned studies helped to prove that the prepared CDs are can be used for biological applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding interaction study of CDs with BSA
ce:section.ce:para: The majority of blood proteins are found in serum albumin, and it is required for the transport and administration of wide range of medicines. BSA has Trp-134 and Trp-213 residues that produce a maximal fluorescence emission peak at 343 nm. It is a common practice to examine the binding mechanism of BSA with CDs and to elucidate the details of binding process via fluorescence behavior of the Trp-residue. In order to gain information on the microenvironment of biomacromolecules and the interaction of nanoparticles with them, steady-state fluorescence spectrometry is typically utilized. The fluorescence spectra of BSA in the absence and presence of different concentrations of RCDs is shown in Fig. 3 a. After being excited at 275 nm, it is found that bovine serum albumin had a significant emission peak at 343nm. In this study, concentration of BSA (3 µM) is kept constant and concentration of CDs is varied from 1−10 µg/mL and the results shows that BSA fluorescence intensity followed a predictable trend like decrease with increasing CDs concentrations. To avoid interruption of CDs fluorescence peak with that of BSA, here we have used 275nm for the excitation of BSA. It is evident from the absorption spectrum measurement that there is much lesser ground state interaction between BSA and RCDs (Fig. S4).

The fluorescence quenching mechanism is examined using Stern-Volmer Eqs. (1) and (2).
(1)
I 0
/ I = 1 +
K SV
[ Q ]
(2)
K SV
=
k q
·
τ 0
where I and I0 are the fluorescence intensities of BSA in the presence and absence of CDs, respectively. The quenching rate constant is kq, quenching constant is KSV, lifetime of BSA (6ns) is τ0 and quencher concentration is [Q]. Fig. 4 a depicts graphs of I0/I against [Q] plotted from the fluorescence analysis data using Eq. (1) and the linear Stern-Volmer plot implies that the fluorescence quenching is dynamic in type. But occasionally this linear pattern can also be seen in static type of ground state complex formation between the fluorophores and the quencher. As a result, the quenching rate constants were determined using the lifetime of BSA and the slope of the linear plot. The calculated values of the KSV and the kq is 1.83×105g/mL and 3.05×1014 (g/mL.s)−1 respectively. The greater value of the dynamic quenching rate constant (kq) in aqueous medium is 2.0×1013 (g/mL.s)−1 [61]. The results showed that the value of quenching rate constant was significantly higher than the reported one, indicating that static quenching mechanism rather than dynamic one that controls the majority of the fluorescence quenching process [62]. This quenching mechanism is further confirmed by time correlated single photon counting techniques, a typical excited state lifetime analysis. In the measurement of lifetime analysis, if a molecule's fluorescence lifetime changes while a second molecule (the quencher) is added, quenching may follow a dynamic type; elsewhile, it may follow a static type [63]. The fluorescence decay curve of the BSA in the presence and absence of CDs is depicted in Fig. 3b. We found that, even at the higher concentration of CDs used for absorption titration, there is no significant alteration in the decay curve or the lifetime of BSA. This may be due to strong binding interaction which occurred between CDs and BSA immediately in ground state. As a result, only the BSA that was not participating in the binding interaction were excited and emitted during the pulse excitation. This finding is highly supports the hypothesis that CDs and BSA are interacting in the ground state and quenching mechanism may follow the static type [64].

It is possible to calculate the number of binding sites (n) and binding constant (Kb) of the BSA-CDs system using the results from fluorescence quenching data and Eq. (3).
(3)
log
(
I 0
− I )
/ I = log
K b
+ n
log
[ Q ]

The values of binding constant and binding sites were determined by linear plot as shown in Fig. 4b. The determined Kb and n values are 5.13×105(g/mL)−1 and 1 respectively. The higher binding constant value exposed the strong binding interaction between BSA and CDs. However, the value of binding site was found 1, indicating that protein binds to CDs as 1:1 ratio.

Molecular docking analysis is conducted to understand the type of molecular interactions between BSA and carbon nanodots at an atomic level. After the fluorescence based experimental confirmation of CDs and BSA binding interaction, molecular docking investigations is carried out to get a deeper understanding of the processes underlying CDs binding to BSA microenvironments. The modelling docking investigation using the CB-Dock technique was performed to further examine the binding affinity and mode between CDs and BSA [65]. Fig. 5 shows the docking confirmation of CDs and BSA system. The blind docking mode with the lowest binding free energy was revealed in Fig. S5. CDs were near Asp-86, His-105, Ser-104, Glu-464, Gln-203, Tyr-147, Arg-196, Lys-106 and Val-461. The following amino acids were mainly found in domain IIA and IIIA of the BSA, which suggested that the location of the interaction was close to domain IIA and IIIA. As shown by the 2D docked structure of BSA-CDs (Fig. 5b), it is ascribed that the CDs was enfolded by H-bonds with the several amino acid residues, such as Glu-464, Gln-203, Tyr-147, Arg-196, Lys-106 and Val-461. The formation of hydrogen bond interaction between CDs and protein decreased the hydrophilicity features and enhanced the hydrophobicity characteristics. As a result, hydrophobicity stabilizes the protein-CDs interactions. Moreover, π-π* stacking and electrostatic interactions between BSA and CDs were also supported by the high binding energies of a few polar amino acid residues. The binding free energy for CDs is −9.62 kcal/mol. The perfect binding of the CDs into the active binding site of BSA suggests that it might be used in bio-related applications.

Visualization of cells and microorganisms have been made ease with the development of fluorescent imaging probes. Nanobiotechnologists are using CDs more for this purpose due to their excellent optical and physical properties. The present study is focused on the application of RCDs as fluorescent imaging probe against the A. cavieae and S.mutans bacterial strains. The CDs was solubilized in PBS solution and utilized for in vitro imaging studies. The bacterial strains were incubated with CDs at 25 °C for 2h. Fig. 8 shows the fluorescence micrographs of CDs with bacteria that were measured at the excitation of 488 and 552nm. The fluorescence images of both A. cavieae and S.mutans were dyed by the RCDs and exhibited diverse fluorescence colors (green and red) at different excitation wavelengths. Both the bacterial strains incubated with CDs showed a significant fluorescence signal that exposed its covenant ability against cell permeability even at briefer incubation time. From the fluorescence images of CDs, it was observed that the strong fluorescence signal was recorded mainly over the cell membrane instead of nucleus. In conflict, no fluorescence signals were identified in the control cells without CDs probe, as shown in Fig. S6. From the above observed results, it clearly revealed that CDs could be utilized as potential fluorescent probe for in-vitro bioimaging applications.

In this work, naturally occurring biomass-derived fluorescent carbon dots was reported. The CDs had great binding affinity with BSA and high binding constants. The CDs served as a powerful quencher, as seen by the fluorescence quenching findings, and the observed quenching pattern was a static one as opposed to the dynamic type and is supported by greater quenching rate constant and lifetime results. The antibiofilm ability of the produced CDs has also been tested against two bacterial strains, including Aeromonas caviae and Streptococcus mutans. According to the findings, both bacteria cell walls have distinct compositions, and CDs have demonstrated a greater decrease in A. caviae biofilms. Moreover, CDs exhibited high fluorescence signal which has the ability to spread throughout the cell membrane rather than in the nucleus. Therefore, CDs may be utilized as a good fluorescent material for in-vitro bio-imaging applications. These characteristics make the synthesized CDs suitable for use in therapeutic applications in the near future.

Rekha Yamini Kosuru: Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Venkatesan Srinivasan: Data curation, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Mariadoss Asha Jhonsi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: M.A.J. acknowledge BSACIST for the seed grant (Reference No: CSD/CSM/2022/08, Dt: 14–07–2022). K.R.Y. acknowledges Mr. K. Sakthivel, PG student, for his assistance in benchmark experiments. V. S greatly acknowledges Vel Tech Rangarajan Dr. Sagunthala R&D Institute of Science and Technology, Chennai-600 062, India, for providing the SEED fund (File No: VTU SEED (FY-22–23)−07). V.S. also acknowledges Dr. A. Kathiravan, Professor, Department of Chemistry, Centre for Molecular Photoscience, Vel Tech Research Park, for the spectral measurements.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2023.137155.
